Meaning / Etymology: To draw together — all things draw close for warmth
Description: Beginning of the Māori new year marked by the rising of Matariki. A time for reflection, remembrance, and setting intentions.
Key Stars Visible: Matariki (Pleiades) — heliacal rising; Puanga/Puaka (Rigel); Takurua (Sirius); Tautoru (Orion's Belt); Atutahi (Canopus); Mahutonga (Southern Cross)
Whakataukī: Ka puta Matariki, ka rere Whānui — ko te tohu tēnā o te tau hou
Whakataukī Translation: When Matariki rises and Whānui trails — that is the sign of the new year
Planting & Gathering: Rest the earth; harvest remaining root crops; gather firewood; collect rongoā
Fishing & Hunting: Inshore fishing on good lunar nights; hīnaki (eel traps) in rivers; preserve fish; kererū season in some regions
Cultural Observances: Matariki celebrations and remembrance; honour those who have passed; set aspirations; communal feasting
Seasonal Notes: Shortest days; weaving, carving, storytelling; strengthen whānau bonds; plan for seasons ahead

Meaning / Etymology: The earth is very cold and hard
Description: Winter deepens but Matariki is established in the sky. Indoor arts flourish — weaving, carving, and sharing of oral traditions.
Key Stars Visible: Matariki (established in pre-dawn sky); Takurua (Sirius) — dominant; Tautoru (Orion's Belt); Rehua (Antares) — low; Whānui (Vega); Mahutonga (Southern Cross)
Whakataukī: He aha te mea nui o te ao? He tangata, he tangata, he tangata
Whakataukī Translation: What is the greatest thing in the world? It is people, it is people, it is people
Planting & Gathering: Earth is dormant; maintain food stores; gather winter greens; prepare seed for spring
Fishing & Hunting: Tuna (eel) season continues; fish on Tangaroa and Rākaunui nights; set hīnaki in estuaries; bird snaring
Cultural Observances: Whare wānanga active; oral traditions passed down; karakia and waiata practice; strengthen community ties
Seasonal Notes: Coldest period; prime time for weaving and fibre arts; carving and tool-making; dream interpretation and spiritual work

Meaning / Etymology: Vegetation beginning to spring up from the earth
Description: Winter loosens its hold. The land stirs with early signs of renewal — a threshold month between cold and warmth.
Key Stars Visible: Matariki (setting in west); Rehua (Antares) — rising; Whakaahu (Castor & Pollux); Whānui (Vega) — high; Mahutonga (Southern Cross) — low south
Whakataukī Translation: Although it is small, it is precious (like greenstone)
Planting & Gathering: Early soil preparation; clear garden plots; first tentative plantings; gather early spring greens
Fishing & Hunting: Coastal fishing improves; whitebait runs may begin; continue tuna harvesting; check bird snaring grounds
Cultural Observances: Prepare for spring ceremonies; final winter wānanga; begin outdoor communal activities
Seasonal Notes: Days lengthening; changeable weather; transition from indoor to outdoor work; energy of renewal building

Meaning / Etymology: The earth has regained its warmth
Description: True spring — life returns vigorously. Birds begin nesting, trees blossom, gardens are prepared in earnest.
Key Stars Visible: Rehua (Antares) — prominent; Whānui (Vega) — high overhead; Mahutonga (Southern Cross); Atutahi (Canopus)
Whakataukī: Kia mau ki te aka matua, kei mau ki te aka tāepa
Whakataukī Translation: Cling to the main vine, not the loose one
Planting & Gathering: Kūmara planting begins (guided by Whānui); plant taro, hue, uwhi; gather spring greens; tend forest gardens
Fishing & Hunting: Inanga (whitebait) runs peak; coastal fishing active; shellfish gathering resumes; freshwater fisheries productive
Cultural Observances: Spring equinox ceremonies; karakia for garden planting; prepare for summer expeditions
Seasonal Notes: Days and nights equal; active outdoor work begins; energy and warmth increasing; bird song fills mornings

Meaning / Etymology: The turning of the earth toward warmth
Description: Earth has become quite warm. Garden planting in full swing. The land is alive with growth and activity.
Key Stars Visible: Rehua (Antares) — high; Scorpius prominent; Mahutonga (Southern Cross); Atutahi (Canopus); Whānui (Vega) — setting west
Whakataukī: Mā te huruhuru ka rere te manu
Whakataukī Translation: With feathers, the bird can fly
Planting & Gathering: Main kūmara planting season; tend growing crops; gather ripening berries and fruits; rongoā plants abundant
Fishing & Hunting: Open ocean fishing active; shellfish plentiful at low tides; net fishing in harbours; rivers productive
Cultural Observances: Garden ceremonies and karakia; preparations for summer gatherings; maintain garden tapu protocols
Seasonal Notes: Long warm days; peak activity season; travel between settlements; abundance building

Meaning / Etymology: The turning of the sky — sun reaches turning point upward
Description: Earth warms fully. Long days stretch toward summer solstice. Gardens thrive and the ocean teems.
Key Stars Visible: Rehua (Antares) — dominant; Mahutonga (Southern Cross) — low south; Atutahi (Canopus); Takurua (Sirius) — beginning to rise east
Whakataukī: E kore e taea te whenu kotahi ki te raranga i te whāriki
Whakataukī Translation: The tapestry of understanding cannot be woven by one strand alone
Planting & Gathering: Crops growing strongly; harvest early-maturing plants; berries and fruits abundant; kaimoana gathering at its best
Fishing & Hunting: Deep sea fishing expeditions; hāpuku and reef fish; crayfish season; fish preservation
Cultural Observances: Summer solstice approaching; large gatherings and hui; inter-hapū visits and trade
Seasonal Notes: Longest days approaching; maximum daylight; warm enough for travel; clear nights for star observation

Meaning / Etymology: Birds are now sitting on their eggs
Description: Peak of summer energy — longest days, warmest nights. The land is generous with food. Full summer (Raumati).
Key Stars Visible: Rehua (Antares) — past zenith going west; Takurua (Sirius) — rising prominently; Tautoru (Orion's Belt) — rising; Atutahi (Canopus); Mahutonga (Southern Cross)
Whakataukī: He manako te kōura i kore ai
Whakataukī Translation: Wishing for crayfish won't bring them — you must go and get them
Planting & Gathering: Crops in full growth; harvest early kūmara varieties; berries and fruits at peak; gather harakeke for weaving
Fishing & Hunting: Peak ocean fishing season; shark fishing expeditions; kōura (crayfish) abundant; kaimoana preservation
Cultural Observances: Summer solstice — longest day; large inter-tribal gatherings; trade and exchange; celebrations and sports
Seasonal Notes: Peak summer warmth; extended daylight; swimming and water activities; abundance and plenty

Meaning / Etymology: The pale colour of maturing vegetation — fruits ripening
Description: Summer's bounty at its fullest. Harvest intensifies as crops reach maturity and the ocean provides generously.
Key Stars Visible: Takurua (Sirius) — dominant overhead; Tautoru (Orion's Belt) — high; Puanga (Rigel); Atutahi (Canopus); Whakaahu (Castor & Pollux)
Whakataukī: Nāu te rourou, nāku te rourou, ka ora ai te iwi
Whakataukī Translation: With your food basket and my food basket, the people will thrive
Planting & Gathering: Main harvest season for kūmara; preserve root crops; gather and dry berries; karaka berries processed
Fishing & Hunting: Ocean fishing continues strongly; net large catches for preservation; dry and smoke fish; coastal foraging excellent
Cultural Observances: Harvest thanksgiving; large hui and wānanga; inter-tribal exchanges
Seasonal Notes: Days beginning to shorten; warmest temperatures; focus on food preservation; prepare stores for autumn/winter

Meaning / Etymology: The assembling together — gathering in of the harvest
Description: Summer crops brought in. Warmth lingers but the shift toward autumn is felt in the evening air.
Key Stars Visible: Takurua (Sirius) — past zenith; Tautoru (Orion's Belt); Atutahi (Canopus) — prominent; Mahutonga (Southern Cross) — rising; Whakaahu (Castor & Pollux) — high
Whakataukī Translation: There is food at the end of my hands — I am self-sufficient
Planting & Gathering: Final harvest — clear remaining crops; store kūmara in rua; preserve and dry foods; gather materials for winter crafts
Fishing & Hunting: Strong fishing continues; intensive fish preservation; shellfish gathering; begin eel trapping preparation
Cultural Observances: Harvest completion ceremonies; gratitude for the season's bounty; prepare for quieter months
Seasonal Notes: Days shortening; evening temperatures cooling; autumn equinox approaching; transition time

Meaning / Etymology: The pillar of the sky — autumn equinox
Description: Day and night stand in balance. Harvests complete, focus shifts to preservation and preparation. The earth begins its turn toward rest.
Key Stars Visible: Mahutonga (Southern Cross) — high south; Atutahi (Canopus) — prominent; Takurua (Sirius) — setting west; Rehua (Antares) — low east
Whakataukī: Whāia te iti kahurangi; ki te tūohu koe, me he maunga teitei
Whakataukī Translation: Seek the treasure you value most; if you bow your head, let it be to a lofty mountain
Planting & Gathering: All crops harvested and stored; garden areas rested; gather autumn foods (fungi, roots); collect seeds for next season
Fishing & Hunting: Tuna (eel) migration begins — prime trapping; hīnaki set; pātiki (flounder) season; bird snaring preparations
Cultural Observances: Autumn equinox observance; reflect on achievements; begin preparations for winter rituals
Seasonal Notes: Days and nights in balance; temperatures cooling; transition to indoor activities; completion and gratitude

Meaning / Etymology: Dried stems and stalks left in gardens after harvest
Description: Landscape grows quieter. Preparation for winter intensifies. The dried stalks stand as testament to the harvest past.
Key Stars Visible: Mahutonga (Southern Cross) — prominent high; Atutahi (Canopus); Rehua (Antares) — rising east; Whānui (Vega) — rising
Whakataukī: Kia hora te marino, kia whakapapa pounamu te moana
Whakataukī Translation: May peace be widespread, may the sea glisten like greenstone
Planting & Gathering: Gardens fully at rest; check and maintain food stores; gather remaining wild foods; collect firewood
Fishing & Hunting: Prime tuna (eel) season; lamprey runs in some rivers; kererū snaring begins; preserve all catches for winter
Cultural Observances: Prepare for Matariki season; remembrance planning; community preparations for winter
Seasonal Notes: Days noticeably shorter; first frosts; autumn colours; drawing inward — indoor life begins

Meaning / Etymology: The final month before the new year
Description: Anticipation and preparation. Winter approaches, food stores checked, the community looks toward the rising of Matariki to begin the cycle anew.
Key Stars Visible: Whānui (Vega) — high; Mahutonga (Southern Cross); Takurua (Sirius) — rising east; Tautoru (Orion's Belt) — rising; Atutahi (Canopus)
Whakataukī: E hara taku toa i te toa takitahi, engari he toa takitini
Whakataukī Translation: My strength is not mine alone — it is the strength of many
Planting & Gathering: Final firewood gathering; check and reinforce food stores; rongoā collection; earth fully at rest
Fishing & Hunting: Late tuna runs; coastal fishing on calm days; final fish smoking/drying; kererū season continues
Cultural Observances: Watch for Matariki's heliacal rising; prepare for new year celebrations; remember those who have passed; set intentions
Seasonal Notes: Shortest days approaching; cold settling in; community draws close; anticipation of Matariki — the cycle begins again
